context: June 2 — Father's Day June 21 (19 days). Safe publish deadline June 8 = 6 days. School is out for most US families. First week of summer. Baptism season ongoing.

seeds.title: Why Does My Child Say 'I Hate You'? (And Why It Actually Means the Opposite)
target_keywords: toddler says I hate you mommy
what to do when child says they hate you
why does my kid say they hate me
child says I hate you what to do
seeds.hook: She was three. The bath had been fine. The towel was blue instead of yellow. And then she said it — 'I hate you, Mommy.' What I didn't know: those three words were proof of something I'd spent three years building.
seeds.alreadyloved_angle: Children say 'I hate you' to the people they feel safest with. It's a belonging test — 'Am I still yours even when I'm like this?' The child who can scream is the child who trusts the relationship can hold anything. Secure attachment produces MORE intense emotional expression, not less. The quiet, compliant child has often learned to protect the relationship by suppressing.
seeds.seo_notes: High-volume, emotionally charged search. Parents Google this mid-crisis. AEO potential: direct answer in first paragraph for featured snippet. No guilt angle. Reframe that feels like grace.

seeds.title: Summer Bedtime Routine for Kids: The Part That Actually Matters
target_keywords: how to handle bedtime in summer
seeds.hook: Every summer I try to figure out what bedtime actually is now. The sun doesn't set until 8:30. Someone needs a snack. Someone found a bug. The routine I built in October is completely gone. Here's what I've learned to hold onto.
seeds.alreadyloved_angle: Bedtime routines feel safe to children not because of schedule but because of ritual — the repeated, predictable coming-together that says 'we always come back to each other.' Strip away the rigidity, keep the connection markers. The bath, the book, the same words said the same way. That's what they need — not the 7pm start time.
seeds.seo_notes: Searched heavily in June as parents lose structure. Different from May 28 summer reading seed — this is about the routine, not the books.

seeds.title: The First Summer She Knew She Was Already Loved
seeds.hook: We didn't go anywhere big. A lake house for four days, a sprinkler in the backyard, a lot of popsicles. But something had changed in her since the fall. She had stopped performing.
seeds.alreadyloved_angle: Narrative brand piece. What it looks like when a child lives from identity rather than performance — the specific, small observable things. She stops trying so hard to be funny. She tells the truth when something isn't fun. She cries when she needs to, stops when she's done. This is Seed → Soil → Fruit shown in a summer story.
seeds.seo_notes: Brand-defining. Not primarily SEO but carries identity keywords for long-tail.

seeds.title: Personalized Gifts for a New Baby That Aren't Just Cute — They're Meaningful
target_keywords: gifts for new baby that last
seeds.hook: You want to give something that says: I already know this child is a specific person. Not just a baby in general. This particular one, with this particular name, who will grow into this particular life.
seeds.alreadyloved_angle: A personalized book isn't a cute keepsake — it's a first message. Before they can read, someone chose to put their name in a story. That matters. Soft CTA territory — positions AlreadyLoved as the answer to 'what do you get a baby you actually want to mean something to?'
seeds.seo_notes: Baptism season + summer baby showers. High commercial intent. Different from May 29 baptism gifts (that was event-specific); this is broader gifting intent.

seeds.title: What I Want My Kids to Remember About This Summer (It Has Nothing to Do With the Vacation)
seeds.hook: We're not going anywhere big this summer. I've been sitting with that, and instead of feeling guilty, I've been asking a different question: what do I actually want them to carry out of these months? And the answer surprised me.
seeds.alreadyloved_angle: What children remember is not the expensive vacation but the ordinary rituals — the same song at bedtime, the popsicles on the porch, being called by the right name when they walked through the door. Memory is made in repetition and presence. Frame: you don't have to give them everything. You have to give them yourself.
seeds.seo_notes: High relatable-engagement value. Parents at the start of summer feeling pressure about 'making it count.' Reduces pressure, increases presence.

seeds.title: What Your Daughter Is Learning About Her Worth by Watching You
seeds.hook: He didn't know she was watching. He was just washing the dishes. But she was watching the way he looked at her mother when she walked into the room. And she was filing it — quietly, without words — into her picture of what she deserves.
seeds.alreadyloved_angle: A daughter's first sense of her own worth comes from how her father sees her. Not in the big speeches, but in the ordinary moments: does he show up? Does he stay? Does he look at her mother like she matters? These moments build her internal picture of what love looks like — and what she'll expect it to look like for the rest of her life.
seeds.urgency: FATHER'S DAY JUNE 21. Safe publish deadline June 8 = 6 DAYS. Approve and publish this week.
seeds.seo_notes: Distinct from May 28 (child's letter to dad), May 29 (dad who doesn't think he's enough), May 30 (letter for invisible work). Fourth angle: dad as identity mirror for daughters.

seeds.title: Best Children's Books for 3-Year-Olds That Are About More Than Bedtime
seeds.hook: Three-year-olds are asking the biggest questions. Who am I? Do I belong? Am I good? The books you put in their hands right now are part of how they start to answer those questions.
seeds.alreadyloved_angle: Different from May 30 '2-year-old books' seed — this is age-3 specific with different developmental hooks. Three is the age of identity formation, 'I can do it myself', beginning of social comparison. Books that name the inner experience matter most here. Leads to personalized book CTA naturally.
seeds.seo_notes: May 30 covered 2-year-olds. This is the 3-year-old version — different search volume, different developmental angle.
